title: SORL1 Microglia Proteomics
text: Total proteomics of human iPSC-derived microglia xenografted into App NL-G-F Alzheimer’s disease mice. Microglia were FACS-sorted as cell pellets and searched against the human proteome (S5 batch, Spectronaut). The primary comparison is SORL1 KO vs WT (n = 4 per genotype).

title: Initial QC & Data Cleaning — Dom Proteomics (SORL1 xenograft microglia)
text:
Define sample metadata
8 samples from a single S5 mass-spec batch (EXP1.1 cohort). Human iPSC-derived microglia xenografted into App NL-G-F mice; SORL1 genotype WT or KO. HET samples (ds31/32/41/42, EXP1.2) are not present in this file.
Note: BDAG173.1f was labelled ds52 in the study metadata but recorded as ds51 in Spectronaut. It is distinguished here by its plate position (S5-A10).


Code
meta <- data.table(
  FileName  = c(
    "ds12_S5-A1_1_6411",   "ds11_S5-A2_1_6412",
    "ds22_S5-A3_1_6413",   "ds21_S5-A4_1_6414",
    "ds51_S5-A9_1_6421",   "ds51_S5-A10_1_6422",
    "ds61_S5-A11_1_6423",  "ds62_S5-A12_1_6424"
  ),
  ShortName = c(
    "BDAG171.1c", "BDAG171.1d",
    "BDAG171.1a", "BDAG171.1b",
    "BDAG173.1e", "BDAG173.1f",
    "BDAG173.1b", "BDAG173.1a"
  ),
  SampleNum = 1:8,
  Genotype  = c("WT", "WT", "WT", "WT", "KO", "KO", "KO", "KO"),
  Sex       = c("Male", "Male", "Male", "Male", "Female", "Female", "Male", "Male"),
  CellCount = c(154300, 143900, 463400, 69340, 141800, 209800, 95890, 237400)
)

Extract unique protein-level quantities
PG.Quantity repeats for every fragment row of a protein within a sample — deduplicate to one row per protein–sample pair.

pg_long <- unique(spec_dom[, .(R.FileName, PG.ProteinGroups, PG.ProteinAccessions,
                                PG.Qvalue, PG.Quantity)])

title: Initial QC & Data Cleaning — Dom Proteomics (SORL1 xenograft microglia)
text:
Pivot to wide format (proteins × samples)
Because BDAG173.1e and BDAG173.1f share the R.FileName prefix “ds51”, we join via meta (by plate position / FileName) before pivoting so each column carries a unique ShortName.


Code
pg_long_named <- merge(pg_long, meta[, .(FileName, ShortName)],
                       by.x = "R.FileName", by.y = "FileName")

quant_wide <- dcast(
  pg_long_named[, .(ShortName, PG.ProteinGroups, PG.Quantity)],
  PG.ProteinGroups ~ ShortName,
  value.var = "PG.Quantity"
)

sample_cols <- meta$ShortName

missing_samples <- setdiff(sample_cols, colnames(quant_wide))
if (length(missing_samples) > 0) {
  warning("Missing from data: ", paste(missing_samples, collapse = ", "))
} else {
  cat("All", length(sample_cols), "samples found in wide matrix.\n")
}


All 8 samples found in wide matrix.


Code
quant_data   <- quant_wide[, ..sample_cols]
quant_matrix <- as.matrix(quant_data)
quant_matrix[is.nan(quant_matrix)] <- NA
colnames(quant_matrix) <- meta$ShortName

cat("Proteins in matrix:", nrow(quant_matrix), "\n")


Proteins in matrix: 13017 


Code
cat("Samples in matrix: ", ncol(quant_matrix), "\n")


Samples in matrix:  8

title: Initial QC & Data Cleaning — Dom Proteomics (SORL1 xenograft microglia)
section: Protein ID counts and % missing per sample
text:
Protein ID counts and % missing per sample
With only 8 samples, statistical outlier rules (e.g. 3-MAD) are unreliable — use protein ID count and % missing as complementary metrics. Cell count is shown alongside, as very low input material (BDAG171.1b, 69 K cells) may drive missingness independently of technical QC failure.


Code
total_pool     <- nrow(quant_matrix)
protein_counts <- colSums(!is.na(quant_matrix) & quant_matrix > 0)
na_pct         <- colMeans(is.na(quant_matrix) | quant_matrix == 0) * 100

qc_counts <- data.table(
  ShortName     = names(protein_counts),
  Protein_Count = protein_counts,
  NA_Percent    = na_pct[names(protein_counts)]
)
qc_data <- merge(qc_counts, meta, by = "ShortName", all.x = TRUE)

cat("Total protein pool (union across all samples):", total_pool, "\n\n")

title: Initial QC & Data Cleaning — Dom Proteomics (SORL1 xenograft microglia)
section: Peak quality: area / height ratio proxy
text:
Peak quality: area / height ratio proxy
EG.FWHM is not present in this export — as a proxy we use NormalizedPeakArea / NormalizedPeakHeight at the fragment level (proportional to peak width). A systematic upward shift in one sample flags poor chromatographic performance.


Code
cat("Reading fragment-level columns for peak quality...\n")


Reading fragment-level columns for peak quality...


Code
frag_raw <- fread(
  input_file,
  select = c("R.FileName", "EG.PrecursorId",
             "F.NormalizedPeakArea", "F.NormalizedPeakHeight",
             "F.ExcludedFromQuantification")
)

frag_dom <- frag_raw[R.FileName %in% meta$FileName]
rm(frag_raw); gc()


           used  (Mb) gc trigger  (Mb)  max used  (Mb)
Ncells  1363904  72.9    2430278 129.8   2430278 129.8
Vcells 62712131 478.5  108114753 824.9 107388228 819.4


Code
frag_dom <- merge(frag_dom, meta[, .(FileName, ShortName, Genotype, Sex, SampleNum)],
                  by.x = "R.FileName", by.y = "FileName")

frag_dom <- frag_dom[
  F.ExcludedFromQuantification == FALSE &
    !is.na(F.NormalizedPeakArea) &
    F.NormalizedPeakHeight > 0
]

frag_dom[, Peak_Width_Proxy := F.NormalizedPeakArea / F.NormalizedPeakHeight]

cat("Quantified fragments retained:", nrow(frag_dom), "\n")


Quantified fragments retained: 3185972 


Code
cat("Samples present:              ", uniqueN(frag_dom$ShortName), "\n")


Samples present:               8 




Code
genotype_colours <- c("WT" = "#1b9e77", "KO" = "#d95f02")
sex_shapes       <- c("Male" = 16, "Female" = 17)

p_peak <- ggplot(frag_dom,
                 aes(x = reorder(ShortName, SampleNum[match(ShortName, meta$ShortName)]),
                     y = Peak_Width_Proxy, fill = Genotype)) +
  geom_boxplot(outlier.shape = NA, alpha = 0.8) +
  coord_cartesian(ylim = quantile(frag_dom$Peak_Width_Proxy, c(0.01, 0.99), na.rm = TRUE)) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = genotype_colours) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "Peak Width Proxy per Sample (NormalisedArea / NormalisedHeight)",
       subtitle = "Higher = broader peaks. Proper FWHM requires re-export with EG.FWHM column.",
       x = "Sample", y = "Area / Height ratio") +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))

print(p_peak)











Code
peak_summary <- frag_dom[, .(
  Median_Width_Proxy = median(Peak_Width_Proxy, na.rm = TRUE),
  P25 = quantile(Peak_Width_Proxy, 0.25, na.rm = TRUE),
  P75 = quantile(Peak_Width_Proxy, 0.75, na.rm = TRUE)
), by = .(ShortName, Genotype, Sex)]

peak_summary <- merge(peak_summary, qc_data[, .(ShortName, SampleNum)], by = "ShortName")

p_peak_dot <- ggplot(peak_summary,
                     aes(x = reorder(ShortName, SampleNum),
                         y = Median_Width_Proxy, color = Genotype, shape = Sex)) +
  geom_point(size = 4) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = P25, ymax = P75), width = 0.3) +
  scale_color_manual(values = genotype_colours) +
  scale_shape_manual(values = sex_shapes) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "Median Peak Width Proxy ± IQR",
       subtitle = "Samples far above the group median have broader / lower-quality peaks",
       x = "Sample", y = "Median Area / Height ratio") +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))

print(p_peak_dot)

title: Initial QC & Data Cleaning — Dom Proteomics (SORL1 xenograft microglia)
text:
Generate QC report


Code
# ── Plot 1: Protein Count per Sample ───────────────────────────────────────
p1 <- ggplot(qc_data,
             aes(x = reorder(ShortName, Protein_Count),
                 y = Protein_Count, fill = Genotype)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = genotype_colours) +
  coord_flip() +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "Total Proteins Identified per Sample",
       x = "Sample", y = "Protein Count")

# ── Plot 2: % Missing per Sample ────────────────────────────────────────────
p2 <- ggplot(qc_data,
             aes(x = reorder(ShortName, NA_Percent),
                 y = NA_Percent, fill = Genotype)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = miss_threshold, color = "red", linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 1) +
  annotate("text", x = 1.5, y = miss_threshold + 1,
           label = paste0(miss_threshold, "% threshold"), color = "red", hjust = 0) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = genotype_colours) +
  coord_flip() +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "% Missing Values per Sample",
       subtitle = "% of the total protein pool absent in each sample",
       x = "Sample", y = "% Missing")

# ── Plot 3: Protein Yield by Genotype with cell count ───────────────────────
p3 <- ggplot(qc_data, aes(x = Genotype, y = Protein_Count, fill = Genotype)) +
  geom_boxplot(outlier.shape = NA, alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_point(aes(shape = Sex, size = CellCount / 1e3), alpha = 0.8,
             position = position_jitter(width = 0.15)) +
  geom_text_repel(aes(label = ShortName), size = 3) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = genotype_colours) +
  scale_shape_manual(values = sex_shapes) +
  scale_x_discrete(limits = c("WT", "KO")) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "Protein Yield by Genotype",
       subtitle = "Point size = cell count (thousands); shape = sex",
       x = "Genotype", y = "Protein Count", size = "Cell count (K)")

# ── PCA ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
cat("Preparing PCA...\n")


Preparing PCA...


Code
pca_mat_log   <- log2(quant_matrix + 1)
pca_mat_log_t <- t(pca_mat_log)

noise_floor <- min(pca_mat_log_t[pca_mat_log_t > 0], na.rm = TRUE) / 2
pca_mat_log_t[is.na(pca_mat_log_t) | pca_mat_log_t == 0] <- noise_floor

valid_cols    <- apply(pca_mat_log_t, 2, var) > 0
pca_mat_log_t <- pca_mat_log_t[, valid_cols]
cat("Proteins retained for PCA (non-zero variance):", sum(valid_cols), "\n")


Proteins retained for PCA (non-zero variance): 12042 


Code
pca_res  <- prcomp(pca_mat_log_t, center = TRUE, scale. = TRUE)
var_expl <- round(100 * pca_res$sdev^2 / sum(pca_res$sdev^2), 1)

pca_df <- as.data.frame(pca_res$x)
pca_df$ShortName <- rownames(pca_df)
pca_df <- merge(pca_df, meta, by = "ShortName", all.x = TRUE)
pca_df <- merge(pca_df, qc_data[, .(ShortName, NA_Percent)], by = "ShortName")

# ── Plot 4: PCA by Genotype ─────────────────────────────────────────────────
p4 <- ggplot(pca_df, aes(x = PC1, y = PC2, color = Genotype, shape = Sex)) +
  geom_point(size = 4, alpha = 0.85) +
  geom_text_repel(aes(label = ShortName), size = 3, max.overlaps = 20) +
  scale_color_manual(values = genotype_colours) +
  scale_shape_manual(values = sex_shapes) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "PCA of Log2 Protein Intensities",
       subtitle = "Color = SORL1 genotype; shape = sex",
       x = paste0("PC1 (", var_expl[1], "%)"),
       y = paste0("PC2 (", var_expl[2], "%)"))

# ── Plot 5: PCA coloured by % missing ───────────────────────────────────────
p5 <- ggplot(pca_df, aes(x = PC1, y = PC2, color = NA_Percent)) +
  geom_point(size = 4, alpha = 0.85) +
  geom_text_repel(aes(label = ShortName), size = 3, max.overlaps = 20) +
  scale_color_viridis_c(name = "% Missing", option = "plasma") +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "PCA coloured by % Missing",
       subtitle = "If a weak sample drives a PC, it should be removed before MSstats",
       x = paste0("PC1 (", var_expl[1], "%)"),
       y = paste0("PC2 (", var_expl[2], "%)"))

# ── Plot 6: PC3 vs PC4 ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
p6 <- ggplot(pca_df, aes(x = PC3, y = PC4, color = Genotype, shape = Sex)) +
  geom_point(size = 4, alpha = 0.85) +
  geom_text_repel(aes(label = ShortName), size = 3, max.overlaps = 20) +
  scale_color_manual(values = genotype_colours) +
  scale_shape_manual(values = sex_shapes) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "PCA — PC3 vs PC4",
       x = paste0("PC3 (", var_expl[3], "%)"),
       y = paste0("PC4 (", var_expl[4], "%)"))

# ── Save PDF ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
pdf_path <- file.path(results_dir, "Initial_QC_Report.pdf")
cat("Saving PDF:", pdf_path, "\n")

title: Initial QC & Data Cleaning — Dom Proteomics (SORL1 xenograft microglia)
text:
Abundance distributions
Log2 intensity boxplots per sample, ordered by sample number. Flagged samples shown with a red outline.


Code
quant_log_plot <- log2(quant_matrix + 1)
quant_log_plot[quant_log_plot == 0] <- NA

abund_long <- as.data.frame(quant_log_plot) %>%
  mutate(Protein = row_number()) %>%
  pivot_longer(-Protein, names_to = "ShortName", values_to = "Log2_Intensity") %>%
  drop_na() %>%
  left_join(as.data.frame(qc_data), by = "ShortName") %>%
  mutate(Status = ifelse(ShortName %in% failed_runs, "Flagged", "Pass"))

p_abund <- ggplot(abund_long, aes(x = reorder(ShortName, SampleNum),
                                   y = Log2_Intensity, fill = Genotype)) +
  geom_boxplot(aes(color = Status), outlier.shape = NA, lwd = 0.4) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = genotype_colours) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Pass" = "black", "Flagged" = "red")) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "Log2 Abundance per Sample",
       subtitle = "Red outlines = samples exceeding % missing threshold",
       x = "Sample", y = "Log2 Intensity") +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))

print(p_abund)

title: Initial QC & Data Cleaning — Dom Proteomics (SORL1 xenograft microglia)
text:
Correlation heatmap
Samples grouped by genotype then sex so biological structure is visible.


Code
quant_mat_cor <- log2(quant_matrix + 1)
quant_mat_cor[quant_mat_cor == 0] <- NA

cor_mat <- cor(quant_mat_cor, use = "pairwise.complete.obs")

meta_sorted    <- meta[order(Genotype, Sex, SampleNum)] %>% as.data.frame()
sorted_names   <- meta_sorted$ShortName
cor_mat_sorted <- cor_mat[sorted_names, sorted_names]

anno_df <- meta_sorted %>%
  select(ShortName, Genotype, Sex) %>%
  as.data.frame()
rownames(anno_df) <- anno_df$ShortName
anno_df$ShortName <- NULL

pheatmap(cor_mat_sorted,
         main              = "Pearson Correlation: Grouped by Genotype × Sex",
         annotation_col    = anno_df,
         annotation_colors = list(
           Genotype = genotype_colours,
           Sex      = c("Male" = "#4393c3", "Female" = "#d6604d")
         ),
         cluster_rows      = FALSE,
         cluster_cols      = FALSE,
         show_rownames     = TRUE,
         show_colnames     = TRUE,
         color             = colorRampPalette(c("navy", "white", "firebrick3"))(100),
         gaps_col          = which(diff(as.numeric(as.factor(meta_sorted$Genotype))) != 0),
         gaps_row          = which(diff(as.numeric(as.factor(meta_sorted$Genotype))) != 0))

Average-correlation diagnostic
Identifies the sample with the lowest mean pairwise correlation.

Values >50% suggest a very low-complexity sample or dominant contaminants (e.g. keratins, actin).

calc_top_n_pct <- function(column, n = 10) {
  sorted_vals <- sort(column, decreasing = TRUE, na.last = TRUE)
  (sum(sorted_vals[1:n], na.rm = TRUE) / sum(column, na.rm = TRUE)) * 100
}

title: 3. DE Plots
text:
Volcano plot function
FDR threshold: 0.05. Fold-change threshold: ±0.5 log2FC (configurable). Top 20 significant genes per direction are labelled, ranked by FDR.


Code
volcano_cols <- c(
  "Upregulated"     = "firebrick3",
  "Downregulated"   = "navy",
  "Not Significant" = "grey80"
)

generate_swanky_volcano <- function(msstats_results, dict, comparison_label,
                                    logfc_cutoff = 0.5, graphs_dir = "results") {
  volcano_data <- msstats_results %>%
    filter(Label == comparison_label, !is.na(adj.pvalue), is.finite(log2FC)) %>%
    left_join(dict, by = "Protein") %>%
    mutate(
      Gene   = ifelse(is.na(Gene) | Gene == "", Protein, Gene),
      Status = case_when(
        adj.pvalue < 0.05 & log2FC >  logfc_cutoff ~ "Upregulated",
        adj.pvalue < 0.05 & log2FC < -logfc_cutoff ~ "Downregulated",
        TRUE                                        ~ "Not Significant"
      )
    ) %>%
    group_by(Status) %>%
    mutate(GroupRank = rank(adj.pvalue, ties.method = "first")) %>%
    ungroup() %>%
    mutate(
      PlotLabel = case_when(
        Status != "Not Significant" & GroupRank <= 20 ~ Gene,
        TRUE                                          ~ NA_character_
      )
    )

  p <- ggplot(volcano_data,
              aes(x = log2FC, y = -log10(adj.pvalue),
                  fill = Status, color = Status)) +
    geom_point(aes(size = Status, alpha = Status), shape = 21, stroke = 0.2) +
    scale_fill_manual(values = volcano_cols) +
    scale_color_manual(values = c(
      "Upregulated"     = "black",
      "Downregulated"   = "black",
      "Not Significant" = "grey90"
    )) +
    scale_size_manual(values = c(
      "Upregulated"     = 3,
      "Downregulated"   = 3,
      "Not Significant" = 1.5
    )) +
    scale_alpha_manual(values = c(
      "Upregulated"     = 1,
      "Downregulated"   = 1,
      "Not Significant" = 0.3
    )) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = -log10(0.05),
               linetype = "dashed", color = "grey30", linewidth = 0.8) +
    geom_vline(xintercept = c(-logfc_cutoff, logfc_cutoff),
               linetype = "dashed", color = "grey30", linewidth = 0.8) +
    geom_label_repel(
      aes(label = PlotLabel),
      fill = "white", color = "black", fontface = "bold", size = 3.5,
      box.padding = 0.5, point.padding = 0.5,
      min.segment.length = 0, segment.color = "grey50", max.overlaps = 50
    ) +
    labs(
      title    = paste0("Differential Abundance: ", gsub("_", " ", comparison_label)),
      subtitle = paste0("Top 20 Genes Labeled | FDR < 0.05 | |log2FC| > ", logfc_cutoff),
      x        = bquote(bold("Log"[2] ~ "Fold Change")),
      y        = bquote(bold("-Log"[10] ~ "FDR"))
    ) +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
    theme(
      plot.title       = element_text(face = "bold", size = 18, hjust = 0),
      plot.subtitle    = element_text(size = 12, color = "grey40",
                                      margin = margin(b = 10)),
      axis.title       = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14),
      axis.text        = element_text(face = "bold", color = "black"),
      axis.line        = element_line(color = "black", linewidth = 1),
      axis.ticks       = element_line(color = "black", linewidth = 1),
      legend.position  = "top",
      legend.title     = element_blank(),
      legend.text      = element_text(size = 12, face = "bold"),
      panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
      panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "grey95")
    )

  dir.create(graphs_dir, showWarnings = FALSE, recursive = TRUE)
  safe_name <- gsub(" ", "_", comparison_label)

  ggsave(
    filename = file.path(graphs_dir, paste0("Volcano_", safe_name, ".pdf")),
    plot     = p,
    width    = 10, height = 7, dpi = 300,
    device   = cairo_pdf
  )

  export_data <- volcano_data %>%
    dplyr::select(Protein, Gene, Description, log2FC, pvalue, adj.pvalue, Status) %>%
    arrange(adj.pvalue)

  fwrite(export_data,
         file = file.path(graphs_dir, paste0("Volcano_Data_", safe_name, ".csv")))

  return(p)
}

title: 3. DE Plots
text:
One-condition-missing proteins
Proteins with issue == "oneConditionMissing" are detected only in WT or only in KO. MSstats assigns them log2FC = ±Inf and excludes them from the volcano (no finite x-coordinate). They are reported here as a separate presence/absence table.
The number of samples in which the protein was detected (from the cleaned pre-imputation Spectronaut data) is shown so genuine on/off candidates (≥3 of 4 samples in the present condition) can be separated from sparse detections that likely reflect noise.


Code
clean_meta <- fread(file.path(objects_dir, "clean_metadata.csv"))

# Detection count per protein per genotype, from the cleaned (pre-imputation)
# fragment-level data. PG.Quantity is the same for every fragment of a given
# (sample, protein) pair, so a protein is "detected" in a sample if any row
# has non-NA PG.Quantity.
detection_counts <- clean_spec %>%
  filter(!is.na(PG.Quantity)) %>%
  distinct(PG.ProteinGroups, R.FileName) %>%
  left_join(
    clean_meta %>% dplyr::select(FileName, Genotype),
    by = c("R.FileName" = "FileName")
  ) %>%
  group_by(PG.ProteinGroups) %>%
  summarise(
    n_WT = sum(Genotype == "WT"),
    n_KO = sum(Genotype == "KO"),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

one_cond <- res_df %>%
  filter(issue == "oneConditionMissing") %>%
  left_join(protein_dictionary, by = "Protein") %>%
  left_join(detection_counts, by = c("Protein" = "PG.ProteinGroups")) %>%
  mutate(
    n_WT      = ifelse(is.na(n_WT), 0L, n_WT),
    n_KO      = ifelse(is.na(n_KO), 0L, n_KO),
    Direction = case_when(
      log2FC ==  Inf ~ "KO-only",
      log2FC == -Inf ~ "WT-only",
      TRUE           ~ NA_character_
    ),
    n_present  = pmax(n_WT, n_KO),
    Confidence = case_when(
      n_present == 4 ~ "High (4/4)",
      n_present == 3 ~ "Moderate (3/4)",
      n_present == 2 ~ "Low (2/4)",
      n_present == 1 ~ "Very low (1/4)",
      TRUE           ~ "None"
    )
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(n_present), Direction, Gene) %>%
  dplyr::select(Protein, Gene, Description, Direction,
                n_WT, n_KO, n_present, Confidence)

fwrite(one_cond, file.path(results_dir, "OneConditionMissing_KO_vs_WT.csv"))

msstats_input_filt <- as.data.frame(msstats_input) %>%
  group_by(ProteinName, PeptideSequence, PrecursorCharge,
           FragmentIon, ProductCharge, Condition) %>%
  summarise(
    n_obs   = sum(!is.na(Intensity) & Intensity > 0),
    n_total = n(),
    prop    = n_obs / n_total,
    .groups = "drop"

  group_by(ProteinName, PeptideSequence, PrecursorCharge,
           FragmentIon, ProductCharge) %>%
  filter(any(prop >= 0.5)) %>%

  inner_join(

title: 2. MSstats — Differential Abundance Analysis
text:
Build contrast matrix
Single comparison: KO vs WT. Positive log2FC = higher abundance in KO.


Code
make_contrast <- function(numerator, denominator, levels) {
  v <- setNames(rep(0L, length(levels)), levels)
  v[numerator]   <-  1L
  v[denominator] <- -1L
  v
}

comparison_matrix <- rbind(
  make_contrast("KO", "WT", condition_levels)
)
rownames(comparison_matrix) <- "KO_vs_WT"

title: 4. FASTA Audit — Mislabelled Search Database
text:
The Spectronaut report delivered as 20260423_094931_S5-A10_Report_human.tsv was searched against a FASTA file named 20K_HUMAN_1GENE_202210.fasta — implying a human-only proteome database. The output contains ~50 % mouse-origin proteins, which is incompatible with a strictly human FASTA: UniProt accessions are globally unique across organisms, so a clean mouse accession in the output proves the FASTA contains mouse sequences.
The likely facts: 1. The FASTA file is a concatenated human + mouse database (appropriate for a xenograft experiment). 2. The filename suggests human-only, which is misleading. 3. Without species filtering, every downstream analysis is contaminated by host (mouse) proteins — this doubles the multiple-testing burden, distorts median normalisation, and confounds the WT vs KO contrast with sample-to-sample host contamination.
This notebook lays out the evidence and recommends post-search species filtering as the fix.

The setup file states a single search database: 20K_HUMAN_1GENE_202210.fasta. By name alone, this implies a Homo sapiens proteome with one canonical isoform per gene (~20 000 entries).

title: 4. FASTA Audit — Mislabelled Search Database
section: Evidence 2 — UniProt accession identity is species-specific
text:
Evidence 2 — UniProt accession identity is species-specific
UniProt accessions are globally unique across organisms — the same accession never refers to two proteins in two species. Therefore, if the output of a search against a strictly human FASTA contains an accession that, in the UniProt knowledge base, is annotated to a non-human organism, the FASTA cannot have been strictly human.
We test this against the full set of unique protein groups in the MSstats output by mapping the primary accession of each group to (a) org.Hs.eg.db and (b) org.Mm.eg.db.


Code
res <- fread(file.path(results_dir, "Full_GroupComparison_Results.csv"))
prim <- str_extract(res$Protein, "^[^;]+")  # first accession in semicolon-separated group

hs <- !is.na(mapIds(org.Hs.eg.db, prim, "SYMBOL", "UNIPROT", multiVals = "first"))


'select()' returned 1:many mapping between keys and columns


Code
mm <- !is.na(mapIds(org.Mm.eg.db, prim, "SYMBOL", "UNIPROT", multiVals = "first"))


'select()' returned 1:many mapping between keys and columns


Code
species_summary <- data.table(
  Category   = c("Human-only mapping",
                 "Mouse-only mapping",
                 "Both species mapping",
                 "Neither species mapping"),
  N_proteins = c(sum( hs & !mm),
                 sum(!hs &  mm),
                 sum( hs &  mm),
                 sum(!hs & !mm))
)

The split is approximately 50 % human / 50 % mouse, not the ~100 % human composition that a human-only FASTA would produce. Zero proteins map to both species, ruling out cross-species ambiguity as the explanation — every mouse-mapped accession is unambiguously mouse.

title: 4. FASTA Audit — Mislabelled Search Database
text:
Evidence 4 — Parallel _mouse report exists
The input directory also contains 20260423_094931_S5-A10_Report_mouse.tsv, searched against uniprotkb_proteome_UP000000589_AND_revi_2025_04_18.fasta (UniProt proteome ID UP000000589 is Mus musculus). The two reports have identical row counts (6 156 104) and were generated from the same raw spectra at adjacent times.
This is consistent with the standard xenograft-proteomics pattern of running two complementary searches — but it does not change the substance of the audit: the report labelled _human does not contain only human protein assignments.

title: 4. FASTA Audit — Mislabelled Search Database
text:
Conclusion

The file 20K_HUMAN_1GENE_202210.fasta used for the _human Spectronaut search contains both human and mouse protein sequences, despite its name.
The proteomics facility’s deliverable is misleading: a user taking the filename at face value would assume a human-only analysis, but every analysis run on this output is a 50 / 50 chimera unless mouse proteins are explicitly filtered post-search.
For a xenograft experiment a mixed FASTA is the correct setup. The issue is the labelling and documentation, not the search itself.

title: 4. FASTA Audit — Mislabelled Search Database
text:
Recommendations

Immediate (this project): filter mouse-origin proteins from the MSstats input prior to dataProcess so normalisation, statistical testing, and FDR are computed on human proteins only. This is implemented in notebook 02_msstats.qmd.
For future deliverables: request the facility to either rename the FASTA file to reflect its true content (e.g. HUMAN_MOUSE_combined_*.fasta) or supply an unambiguously human-only FASTA for searches whose stated purpose is human-only.
Reproducibility: retain both _human and _mouse reports for transparency. The _mouse report is searched against a verifiable mouse proteome (UP000000589) and could support a future host-response analysis if needed.

Human iPSC-derived microglia xenografted into App NL-G-F Alzheimer’s disease mice. Microglia FACS-sorted as cell pellets; SORL1 genotype WT or KO (HET not in this batch).
